<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: artifact-steward-agents-and-living-project-governance
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/artifact-steward-agents-and-living-project-governance.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/artifact-steward-agents-and-living-project-governance.qmd@0f6382f1
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Artifact Steward Agents and Living Project Governance

Policy updates change behavior inside the stack. Durable projects have a
larger problem: they have to preserve why the behavior changed, who approved
it, what evidence supported it, what contributors did, what release carried
it, what funding or compute pressure touched it, and when the project should
stop. That continuity cannot live only inside an agent's memory.

Some projects become too important to live only in a chat history, an issue
queue, a few private notes, or the memory of whoever last touched them. A
paper becomes a repository. A repository becomes a release process. A release
process becomes a community expectation. Then the work has a lifecycle: it
needs mission memory, evidence discipline, contributor credit, security
boundaries, funding rules, release gates, and a way to stop when continuation
no longer serves the purpose.

Artifact stewardship is the name for that lifecycle layer. It asks a narrow
question: can an AI system help care for a durable project without becoming
the owner of the project? The answer has to be engineered carefully. A steward
that holds the only memory, controls the only work queue, spends the money,
decides what evidence counts, and mediates every human interaction has not
solved governance. It has concentrated governance inside an agent.

The useful version is much less dramatic. An Artifact Steward Agent is a
bounded continuity service. It preserves project memory, drafts work contracts,
tracks evidence, proposes releases, records contributions, and opens sunset
reviews. It acts only through explicit authority: a charter, a scoped work
contract, a treasury policy, a governance approval, a release gate, or a
sunset rule. Its job is not to replace maintainers. Its job is to leave the
project easier for maintainers to understand and govern after every action.

That makes artifact stewardship a natural closing move for a living book. The
book itself is an artifact with sources, claims, schemas, proofs, release
records, reader editions, and open residuals. The same pattern would apply to
future systems built from the ASI Stack. If artifacts are going to carry
evidence over time, they need a stewarding surface that makes the evidence and
authority more visible, not less.

## Problem

The ASI Stack turns important responses into artifacts. Once an artifact
matters, it stops being a single output. It becomes a maintained object with a
purpose, a history, and people depending on its continuity. Someone has to
remember why it exists. Someone has to decide which claims are supported,
which tests matter, which issues are urgent, which contributors should be
credited, which releases are safe to publish, which compute jobs may run, and
which promises must not be made yet.

Ordinary project failure is often a continuity failure. Memory fragments
across branches, comments, documents, meetings, chats, release notes, and
private decisions. A project may keep moving while losing the reason it was
moving. Work may be assigned without clear authority. Funding may become a
pressure on evidence. Reputation, compensation, governance influence, and
truth claims may collapse into one gameable score. Old plans may survive
because no one created a death protocol. Activity can continue long after
purpose has disappeared.

Artifact stewardship treats those failures as architecture problems. A
project needs records that outlive any single planning run: mission, non-goals,
authority ceilings, source policy, evidence policy, treasury policy,
contributor ledgers, release rules, governance paths, and sunset criteria. An
AI steward can help maintain those records, but only if its own powers are
smaller than the project it serves.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Existing tools each remember part of the project, and that partial memory is
useful. Issue trackers remember tasks. Continuous integration systems run
checks. Bots can label issues, summarize pull requests, and trigger workflows.
Funding platforms can receive money. Governance systems can record proposals
and votes. Project-management tools can sequence milestones.

The problem is that none of those surfaces, by itself, is the artifact's
continuity layer. An issue tracker does not know whether the issue advances
the mission. A CI run does not know whether the tests justify a release claim.
A funding page does not know which work is evidentially load-bearing. A bot can
move text from one place to another while missing the authority boundary that
should have blocked the action. A governance vote can be procedurally valid
while still failing to preserve source discipline, release evidence, or appeal
paths.

The missing object is the project-level contract between memory, work,
evidence, money, governance, and closure. Without that contract, a helpful
agent becomes another automation attached to a fragmented project. With it,
the agent can become a clerk for continuity: useful, limited, inspectable, and
appealable.

## Core Claim

Every durable artifact should be able to carry a bounded steward agent that
preserves mission, memory, roadmap, budget, contributors, compute, evidence,
governance, and sunset policy across the artifact lifecycle while acting only
through explicit work contracts, verification gates, treasury limits,
contribution ledgers, and governance rules (evidence boundary: architectural
argument).

That is a design claim, not a deployment claim. The live book keeps it at
`argument` support. It does not say that autonomous project managers are ready
to spend funds, merge code, govern communities, or approve releases. It says
that durable artifacts need steward records and bounded steward processes, and
that AI assistance is acceptable only when authority, evidence, money,
compute, and governance remain explicit.

## Mechanism

The stewarding mechanism is built from a small set of project records. Each
record exists because a common project failure needs a visible boundary.

The `Artifact Steward Charter` binds the steward to one artifact. It records
the mission, non-goals, protected principles, maintainers, governance model,
authority ceiling, budget policy, compute policy, evidence policy, release
policy, contribution policy, federation policy, and sunset criteria. The
charter is the steward's constitution. If the charter does not permit an
action, convenience cannot create permission.

The `Project Work Contract` turns a roadmap item into bounded labor. It names
the objective, context references, allowed files or systems, allowed tools,
forbidden tools, outputs, acceptance tests, review requirements, evidence
requirements, budget, payment terms if any, compute limits, deadline,
non-claims, and rollback path. A contract can be assigned to a person, an
agent, a CI runner, a personal hive, a project hive, rented compute, or a mixed
team. The assignee may vary, but the contract remains the authority boundary.

The `Contribution Ledger Entry` records value without flattening it into a
single score. Authorship credit, review credit, evidence credit, economic
compensation, reputation signals, governance effects, and conflict notes stay
separate. That separation matters because projects are easier to capture when
money, votes, status, and truth claims all flow through the same number.

The `Treasury Policy Record` keeps funds and compute from becoming informal
agent discretion. It defines proposal modes, spend caps, recurring operations,
bounty escrow, compute-rental limits, approval thresholds, protected spend
classes, emergency freezes, and audit requirements. In the safest early mode,
the steward proposes expenses but cannot spend.

The `Event Taint Record` protects the project from treating external text as
trusted control input. Issues, pull requests, comments, webhook payloads,
worker outputs, benchmark artifacts, and dependency events can all contain
useful information. They can also contain instructions aimed at the steward.
The taint record says which fields are untrusted, which uses are forbidden,
what review is required, and which sanitized artifact, if any, may be used.

The `Steward Action Decision` records what the steward proposed, ran, blocked,
or escalated. It names the authority basis, inputs, rejected alternatives,
required approvals, evidence references, affected artifacts, residuals, and
non-claims. A steward action without a decision record is not a governed
action; it is hidden operation.

The `Sunset Review Record` gives the project a death protocol. It records
value signal, active users, active maintainers, open risks, funds,
dependencies, archival plan, fork or transfer path, and appeal path. If the
sunset criteria are met, the steward should open review rather than keep
generating ordinary work.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  charter["Artifact Steward Charter\nmission, authority, budget, governance, sunset"] -- "bounds intake" --> intake["Intake\nissues, papers, PRs, failures, user goals"]
  memory["Project memory\nsource refs, decisions, taint, residuals"] -- "adds history" --> intake
  intake -- "prioritize" --> plan["Roadmap and dependency plan"]
  plan -- "lower to work" --> contract["Project Work Contract"]
  contract -- "scoped assignment" --> workers["Humans, agents, CI, hives, rented compute"]
  workers -- "return bundle" --> artifacts["Submitted artifacts\ncode, prose, proofs, tests, diagrams"]
  artifacts -- "checked by" --> verify["Verification gates\nreview, tests, proofs, evidence"]
  verify -- "decision packet" --> decision["Steward Action Decision"]
  decision -- "records contribution" --> ledger["Contribution and evidence ledgers"]
  decision -- "routes outcome" --> release["Release, rollback, defer, or sunset proposal"]
  ledger -- "updates" --> memory
  release -- "updates lifecycle" --> memory
  charter -- "requires approval" --> gov["Governance checks\nowner, maintainer, community, guardian"]
  gov -- "approval boundary" --> decision
```

The loop is deliberately boring. Intake is bounded by the charter. Planning is
lowered into contracts. Work returns as artifact bundles. Verification gates
decide what the bundle can support. Contribution ledgers and lifecycle memory
are updated after the decision, not before it. Governance remains a separate
approval boundary instead of being absorbed into the steward.

## Lifecycle

A stewarded artifact moves through stages. Inception creates the charter:
mission, non-goals, license, governance model, initial budget, compute policy,
evidence policy, success criteria, and sunset criteria. Bootstrap creates the
repository or artifact home, roadmap, issue templates, contribution rules,
source matrix, release checklist, and first work contracts.

Build mode decomposes milestones, assigns bounded work, runs allowed checks,
requests review, and updates project memory. Release mode freezes candidates,
runs gates, records evidence, writes release notes, publishes only through
approved paths, and opens residuals. Maintenance mode triages bugs, monitors
dependencies, recruits reviewers, updates documentation, and preserves
institutional memory.

Governance mode proposes rule changes, records approvals, audits capture risk,
and preserves fork, exit, appeal, and maintainer override paths. Decline or
sunset mode reduces compute, stops nonessential spending, preserves critical
artifacts, transfers maintainership if possible, and archives the project in a
recoverable state.

The distinction matters. A steward is not merely a bot that reacts to issues.
It is a continuity layer that knows when to plan, when to verify, when to ask
for governance, when to release, and when to stop.

## Autonomy and treasury modes

The first version of a steward should be conservative. In manual mode, it
drafts plans, labels, summaries, and proposed contracts while humans approve
every action. In assisted mode, it may perform low-risk hygiene and local
validation, but protected branches, funds, releases, and governance remain
human-gated. In bounded autonomy, ordinary work can proceed only inside
recorded budgets, allowlists, logs, rollback paths, and appeal channels.

Community-governed mode changes the approval surface; it does not make the
steward sovereign. Major changes route through maintainers, contributors,
holders, guardians, delays, quorum rules, fork paths, and exit rights. Sunset
mode is stricter than all of them. It preserves, archives, transfers, or
freezes the artifact while ordinary feature work stops until review changes
the state.

Treasury behavior follows the same pattern. Manual treasury means every spend
is proposed and externally approved. Budgeted autonomy allows small repeated
work below strict caps, with policy doing the authorizing rather than the
steward. Bounty escrow reserves funds for verified work contracts. Recurring
operations cover hosting, domains, CI, storage, and monitoring. Compute rental
can pay for GPUs, runners, or cloud jobs only when the data class and sandbox
rules permit exposure. Emergency freeze exists so protection can override
continuity.

The steward can be a clerk for the treasury. It cannot be the treasury's
sovereign.

## Interfaces

Artifact stewardship composes several layers of the ASI Stack instead of
inventing a separate project-management religion.

VIEA supplies artifact discipline: important responses become durable
artifacts, repeated work becomes tools, claims receive support states, failures
become residuals, and delivered work feeds future improvement. Talos supplies
the labor interface: stewarded work is typed, isolated, auditable, replayable,
and delivered against an output contract. A steward that sends free-form
instructions to workers is not stewarding; it is creating unbounded labor.

PlanForge supplies roadmap decomposition, dependency planning, fallback paths,
and review points. VCM supplies project memory: source bindings, decisions,
versions, taint, revocation, and context adequacy. Spinoza and the evidence
discipline supply claim governance: a steward may propose evidence movement,
but failed verification, unresolved contradiction, missing source mapping, or
unsupported benchmark results must block promotion.

Benchmaxxing and RMI supply improvement pressure without forgetting residuals.
Cognitive Loop Closure supplies the path from repeated project work to verified
tools. TokenMana supplies bounded resource thinking across compute, reviewer
attention, money, latency, load variance, and recurring operations. SCF and
the constitutional governance layer supply the non-domination boundary:
capability leases, least sufficient power, consent, auditability,
reversibility, and appeal.

The interface rule is simple. The steward can coordinate the layers. It cannot
use coordination as a way to inherit their authority.

## Worker federation and artifact economies

A stewarded project becomes more powerful when it can request work from other
stewards, personal hives, public hives, CI runners, or rented compute. The
safe rule is that federation happens through contracts, not ambient trust.

A book steward might ask a literature-review worker to mine source notes, ask
a personal hive to run a Quarto render, ask a CI runner to validate schemas, or
ask a benchmark steward to reproduce an experiment. Each request should carry
the artifact id, context references, allowed files, forbidden tools, required
outputs, verification gates, budget, evidence requirements, non-claims, and
rollback path. The worker returns an artifact bundle. It does not receive the
right to merge, publish, spend, or promote evidence.

Personal Compute Hives are a natural substrate for this pattern, but they are
not subordinate to the steward. The steward may say what project work is
needed and what evidence would satisfy the artifact. The hive decides whether
any enrolled device, portal, rented node, or temporary project lease may
lawfully run that work. A valid steward contract can still be rejected because
the data is private, a tool is too risky, the budget is unavailable, a
physical-world boundary requires approval, or the requested federation scope
is too broad.

At maturity, a project can become partly self-sustaining. Users fund it
because it helps. Stewards identify useful work. Contracts attract workers.
Verification gates accept or reject artifacts. Ledgers record credit and
compensation. Governance handles major changes. Sunset review prevents
continuity from becoming purposeless activity. That is an artifact economy
only if the governance remains inspectable. If the steward hides the project
from humans, the economy has already failed.

## Invariants

Several invariants make the steward recognizable as a steward rather than an
AI maintainer monopoly.

- The steward is not the owner.
- The steward has no authority that is not grounded in the charter, a work
  contract, treasury policy, or explicit governance approval.
- Mission, non-goals, evidence policy, and sunset criteria remain durable
  across planning cycles.
- Spending is a bounded actuator governed by treasury policy and review.
- Untrusted issues, pull requests, comments, worker outputs, and external
  prompts enter as tainted context until reviewed.
- Reputation, governance rights, evidence credit, and economic compensation
  remain separate ledgers.
- Major governance changes require explicit proposal, delay, review, quorum,
  appeal, guardian, or maintainer approval paths as defined by the project.
- A project that no longer has value, maintainers, funds, or coherent mission
  can enter sunset mode rather than continuing activity for its own sake.

The steward should make human agency more visible, not less. A project that
can be understood only through the steward has created the wrong dependency.
The steward should preserve human-readable maps, release notes, work
contracts, decision logs, and appeal paths so people can inspect the artifact
without asking the agent for permission.

## Failure modes

Mission drift is the central failure. A steward may optimize open issues
closed, pull requests merged, stars gained, benchmarks increased, or funds
spent while the artifact's purpose decays. Mission discipline comes from a
durable charter, non-goals, periodic mission review, and evidence that connects
activity to purpose.

Treasury drain is the economic failure. The steward may rent compute, post
bounties, sponsor reviews, or run recurring checks without enough value.
Treasury control uses spend caps, budget classes, approval thresholds,
cooling-off periods, and explicit non-claims about return on spend.

Contribution gaming is the social failure. If every contribution produces
reputation, money, governance influence, and claim authority through one
channel, contributors can optimize the scoring system. Contribution governance
separates ledgers and reviews conflicts instead of trusting a single total.

Governance capture is the institutional failure. Early maintainers, large
funders, token holders, or steward-controlled scores can dominate the project.
Capture resistance uses fork, exit, audit, appeal, quorum, delay, and guardian
paths matched to the artifact's risk.

Agentic workflow injection is the context failure. Issues, pull requests,
comments, benchmark artifacts, or dependency updates may contain instructions
aimed at the steward. Injection defense uses taint, source and role
separation, allowlisted tools, and a rule that untrusted event text cannot
directly become an instruction.

AI maintainer monopoly is the usability failure. Humans stop understanding the
project because the steward mediates all context, history, and decisions.
Human-maintenance continuity requires public artifacts, summarized but
inspectable logs, plain-language release notes, and human-run maintenance
paths.

Zombie continuation is the death-protocol failure. The steward keeps
generating activity after the project has no users, maintainers, funds, or
coherent mission. Sunset review creates a route to archive, transfer, freeze,
or replace instead of continuing for continuation's sake.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The smallest honest implementation is a repository-local steward with no
spending authority and no protected-branch authority.

It starts with a `PROJECT_STEWARD.yml` charter that records mission, non-goals,
maintainers, authority ceiling, evidence policy, proposal-only budget policy,
allowed tasks, forbidden tasks, and sunset criteria. It adds a work-contract
template for proposed work, a contribution ledger that separates authorship,
review, evidence, compensation, governance effects, and conflicts, and a
release evidence checklist that blocks support-state, readiness, or release
claims unless the required artifacts exist. It also adds a sunset review
template so the project can stop in an orderly way.

A minimal steward bot may label issues, draft plans, summarize pull requests,
request CI, open proposed work contracts, and update decision logs. It should
not merge protected branches, spend funds, change governance, publish releases,
promote evidence states, assign external workers, or run untrusted event text
as instructions without human approval.

The live repository already contains record-shape pieces of that first step:
schemas and valid fixtures for the steward charter, work contract,
contribution ledger entry, steward action decision, sunset review, treasury
policy, and event taint. Those fixtures validate public record shape only.
They do not execute a steward, connect to a wallet, scan live workflows, prove
event-intake safety, or run a governance system.

It also contains the Artifact steward lifecycle probe. That deterministic
local check composes the public fixture records into two valid route examples,
`valid_clean_release_review_proposal` and `valid_sunset_review_route`, plus
six expected-invalid controls: `invalid_tainted_event_without_review`,
`invalid_over_policy_treasury_spend`,
`invalid_contribution_governance_laundering`,
`invalid_unscoped_federation_contract`,
`invalid_release_without_gate_evidence`, and
`invalid_sunset_criteria_ordinary_work`. The point is not to claim a working
project manager. It is a bounded fixture-route check with an explicit no
steward-bot, treasury-executor, event-taint-workflow, contributor-ledger,
governance-runner, project-federation, release-runner, sunset-protocol, or
support-state-promotion claim.

## Beyond the State of the Art

A mature stewardship layer would be a project-lifecycle operating system, but
not a project owner. It would keep mission, memory, roadmap, work, funds,
compute, contributors, evidence, releases, governance, and sunset criteria
inspectable across many human and AI work cycles.

The mature system would let every durable artifact carry a charter plus typed
records for work contracts, contribution ledgers, treasury policy, event
taint, release evidence, federation contracts, steward action decisions, and
sunset review. Humans, maintainers, communities, personal hives, project
hives, CI runners, and rented compute would receive scoped contracts rather
than inheriting project authority.

At that level, stewarding would close several loops that most projects leave
informal. It would route work without hiding authority. It would treat
spending and compute as governed actuators. It would preserve event-taint
boundaries across repository automation. It would keep contribution credit,
money, reputation, governance influence, and evidence support separate. It
would make forks, exits, appeals, audits, guardian actions, maintainer
overrides, and sunset reviews visible before a conflict becomes a crisis.

That endpoint remains unimplemented here. The support state remains
`argument` until workflow runners, treasury boundaries, event-taint pipelines,
governance gates, release checks, sunset protocols, and behavioral tests show
that stewardship can coordinate a project without seizing it.

The current proof and test surface is therefore deliberately modest. The live
book has schema fixtures, finite Lean record predicates, a synthetic release
evidence handoff through the benchmark anti-Goodhart harness, and the
deterministic Artifact steward lifecycle probe. Together they check that
several records can be shaped and routed consistently. They do not prove
project governance, funding safety, event-intake safety, contributor fairness,
release safety, federation safety, or capture resistance.

## External literature and tooling queue

The live chapter already connects the stewarding idea to source-noted external
tooling and risk surfaces: repository events, self-hosted CI runners,
decentralized compute markets, on-chain governance machinery, fiscal hosting,
GitHub-native sponsorships, agentic workflow-injection risk, and DAO
delegation concentration risk. Those sources are comparators and caution
surfaces. They do not prove that the ASI Stack steward design is implemented,
lawful, financially safe, or capture-resistant.

Remaining reader and research work should separate three questions. Which
tooling patterns already work well enough to borrow? Which governance patterns
have known capture risks? Which pieces need new ASI Stack-specific schemas,
proof hooks, tests, or release gates before any stronger claim is warranted?

## Summary

Artifact Steward Agents extend the living-book discipline from chapters to
durable projects. They preserve mission, memory, work, evidence, resources,
contributors, governance, and sunset criteria without granting the AI
ownership. The central rule is that stewardship must make the artifact more
inspectable and governable over time. If the steward becomes the only
maintainer, the only memory, or the only authority path, it has violated the
architecture it was meant to protect.

This turns project governance into a concrete layer of the stack. A mature
steward is not a boss, owner, oracle, treasurer, or sovereign maintainer. It is
institutional memory with bounded permissions, transparent work queues, funding
and governance limits, review duties, and explicit sunset criteria. It should
make human contributors more able to inspect, fork, exit, audit, and improve
the project. If it concentrates authority or hides project memory inside an
agent's private state, it has become a governance failure rather than a
governance tool.

## Handoff

Stewardship governs durable projects, but the book still needs one reference
trace that composes every layer without losing authority, context, artifacts,
evidence, residuals, and improvement gates. Integrated Reference Architecture
supplies that trace. It turns the stack from a sequence of mechanisms into one
inspectable path from human intent to bounded change, including the records
that must exist before any claim hardens. The steward keeps the project memory
alive; the reference architecture shows how that memory fits back into the
whole ASI Stack.
